verses.text: David's six sons born in Hebron are named, each by a different mother. The list hints at the strife a divided house would breed. Even a man after God's heart reaped sorrow from his many wives.
David's six sons born in Hebron are named, each by a different mother. The list hints at the strife a divided house would breed. Even a man after God's heart reaped sorrow from his many wives.

The sons born in Jerusalem include Nathan and Solomon, both born of Bathsheba. Out of the place of David's deepest sin, God brought the heir of the promise and the line of the Messiah. Grace writes redemption through repented failure.

The sons born in Jerusalem include Nathan and Solomon, both born of Bathsheba. Out of the place of David's deepest sin, God brought the heir of the promise and the line of the Messiah. Grace writes redemption through repented failure.

The kings of Judah are listed in unbroken succession — Solomon to Rehoboam down to Josiah. Through good kings and evil, God kept a lamp burning for David. The dynasty endured not by merit but by covenant faithfulness.

The kings of Judah are listed in unbroken succession — Solomon to Rehoboam down to Josiah. Through good kings and evil, God kept a lamp burning for David. The dynasty endured not by merit but by covenant faithfulness.

Josiah's sons and grandson carry the line to the very edge of the exile. The throne is about to fall. Yet even as judgment looms, the genealogy keeps the royal line in view, for the promise could not perish.

Josiah's sons and grandson carry the line to the very edge of the exile. The throne is about to fall. Yet even as judgment looms, the genealogy keeps the royal line in view, for the promise could not perish.

After the exile the line continues through Jeconiah to Zerubbabel, who led the return. The royal seed survived Babylon. The dynasty fell, but the heir lived, and with him the unbroken promise of a King to come.

After the exile the line continues through Jeconiah to Zerubbabel, who led the return. The royal seed survived Babylon. The dynasty fell, but the heir lived, and with him the unbroken promise of a King to come.

After the exile the line continues through Jeconiah to Zerubbabel, who led the return. The royal seed survived Babylon. The dynasty fell, but the heir lived, and with him the unbroken promise of a King to come.

The descendants of Zerubbabel are traced for several more generations. For the returned exiles with no king on the throne, this list was hope itself. The line of David was alive, traceable, and still bearing the ancient promise toward Christ.
